title: Embeddings make meaning searchable
text: Embeddings are dense numeric vectors that encode the meaning of text. A model trained on large amounts of text learns to place similar meanings near each other in vector space. For example, car and automobile end up close together even though they share no letters. This property is what makes semantic search possible: you search by meaning, not by exact words.

title: Tokenization before embedding
text: Before a model produces an embedding it breaks the input into tokens. A token is usually a word piece or a short character sequence. Tokenization determines what the model can represent, which is why very long texts need chunking before indexing. The tokenizer vocabulary is fixed at training time.

title: Cosine similarity intuition
text: Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors rather than their length. Two short texts and two long texts about the same topic will have a high cosine similarity even if one is more detailed. A score near 1.0 means the vectors point in nearly the same direction; near 0 means they are unrelated.

title: OpenAI style embedding APIs
text: Cloud embedding APIs such as the OpenAI text-embedding-3-small model accept a string and return a float array. The array has hundreds or thousands of dimensions. Storing these arrays in a vector database enables fast approximate nearest-neighbor lookup at query time. The API handles model updates transparently.

title: SentenceTransformer models
text: SentenceTransformer is an open-source library that wraps pre-trained models for producing sentence-level embeddings. Models like all-MiniLM-L6-v2 run locally, require no API key, and produce 384-dimensional vectors suitable for semantic search and clustering tasks.

title: FAISS is a vector index
text: FAISS is a library from Meta that indexes dense float vectors for fast similarity search. It supports exact search and approximate nearest-neighbor methods. The IndexFlatIP index does exact inner-product search, which is equivalent to cosine similarity after L2 normalization. It handles millions of vectors efficiently.

title: ChromaDB stores embeddings and metadata
text: ChromaDB is an open-source vector store that persists documents, their embeddings, and arbitrary metadata together. A collection in ChromaDB is like a table: you add documents and query by embedding. Filters on metadata let you scope searches by category, date, or any custom field.

title: Chunking improves retrieval
text: Long documents are split into short chunks before embedding because a single embedding cannot capture all the details of a long text. Chunks of 200 to 500 words are a common starting point. Smaller chunks improve recall for specific facts but may lose surrounding context needed for understanding.

title: Chunk overlap preserves context
text: When chunking a long document, adding a 10 to 20 percent overlap between adjacent chunks helps preserve sentences that would otherwise be cut at a boundary. Without overlap, a sentence split across two chunks may be impossible to retrieve as a coherent unit.

title: RAG combines retrieval and generation
text: Retrieval-Augmented Generation combines a retrieval step with a language model generation step. The retriever finds the most relevant passages from a knowledge base, and those passages are injected into the prompt as context before the model generates an answer grounded in that material.

title: Mini-RAG pipeline steps
text: A mini-RAG pipeline usually follows three steps: retrieve the top chunks for the query, augment the prompt with those chunks, and generate the response from the language model. The workflow is simple but powerful and sharply reduces the chance that the model invents unsupported facts.

title: Hallucination is unsupported output
text: Hallucination happens when a language model gives a confident answer that is not supported by any source. In RAG systems this risk is lower because the model reads retrieved context first and is instructed to base its answer on that content rather than on memorized training data.

title: Grounding reduces hallucination
text: Grounding means anchoring a model response in a specific source document. When the prompt explicitly shows retrieved text, the model has less reason to invent details. Grounding does not eliminate hallucination entirely but reduces it significantly in practice.

title: Top-k retrieval
text: Top-k retrieval returns the k most similar chunks for a query. Choosing k involves a trade-off: a small k gives precise context but may miss relevant passages, while a large k adds noise that can dilute the quality of the generated answer. Three to five is a common default.

title: Query rewriting
text: Query rewriting improves retrieval by rephrasing the user question before embedding it. A short keyword query can be expanded into a natural language sentence that matches the style of indexed documents, which increases semantic overlap and retrieval quality.

title: Bi-encoder and cross-encoder
text: A bi-encoder embeds the query and each document independently, making it fast for large corpora. A cross-encoder encodes the query and document together, giving more accurate relevance scores but at much higher computational cost. RAG pipelines often use bi-encoders for retrieval and cross-encoders for reranking.

title: Recall and precision in search
text: Recall measures how many relevant documents were returned out of all relevant documents in the corpus. Precision measures how many returned documents were actually relevant. A good retriever balances both: missing key passages hurts the RAG answer, while including irrelevant ones adds noise to the prompt.

title: Paris is a major city
text: Paris is the capital of France and one of the most visited cities in the world. It is known for the Eiffel Tower, world-class museums, and French cuisine. This document is intentionally off-topic to verify that the retriever correctly ranks it low for AI or finance related queries.

title: How compound interest grows savings
text: Compound interest means you earn returns not just on your original deposit but also on the interest already accumulated. A savings account growing at 6 percent per year doubles roughly every 12 years thanks to compounding. Starting to save early matters far more than the initial amount because time amplifies the effect exponentially.

title: Credit scores explained
text: A credit score is a number between 300 and 850 that lenders use to assess how likely you are to repay a loan on time. Payment history accounts for about 35 percent of the score, making it the single biggest factor. Missing a payment damages the score quickly while rebuilding it requires months of consistent on-time payments.

title: Index funds versus active funds
text: An index fund tracks a market benchmark such as the S&P 500 and charges low fees because no analyst team is picking stocks. An actively managed fund employs portfolio managers to select investments but typically charges higher fees. Decades of research show that most active funds underperform their benchmark index after fees over a ten-year horizon.

title: Budgeting with the 50-30-20 rule
text: The 50-30-20 rule divides after-tax income into three buckets: 50 percent for needs such as rent, utilities, and groceries; 30 percent for wants such as dining out and entertainment; and 20 percent for savings and debt repayment. It is a practical starting framework for anyone building their first household budget.

title: Inflation erodes purchasing power
text: Inflation is the rate at which the general price level rises over time. When annual inflation is 5 percent, a basket of goods that cost 100 dollars last year costs 105 dollars today. Keeping money idle in a low-yield account means the real value of savings quietly shrinks every year.

title: Active recall beats re-reading
text: Active recall is a study technique where you test yourself on material rather than reading it passively. Closing your notes and writing down everything you can remember forces the brain to retrieve information, which strengthens memory traces far more effectively than simply reviewing the same pages again.

title: Spaced repetition schedules review
text: Spaced repetition spreads review sessions across increasing time intervals. Reviewing a concept one day after learning it, then three days later, then a week later, exploits the spacing effect to lock information into long-term memory. It also reduces total study hours compared to cramming the night before an exam.

title: Online courses and self-paced learning
text: Online courses let learners set their own schedule and replay difficult sections as many times as needed. The completion rate for self-paced courses is low because external deadlines are absent. Learners who set personal weekly milestones and join peer study groups finish at a significantly higher rate.

title: Feynman technique for deep understanding
text: The Feynman technique asks you to explain a concept in plain language as if teaching it to someone with no background. Wherever the explanation stumbles is exactly where your understanding has gaps. Returning to the source material to fill those gaps builds a much deeper mental model than re-reading alone.

title: Preventive screenings save lives
text: Preventive health screenings detect diseases before symptoms appear, when treatment is most effective. Blood pressure checks, cholesterol panels, and colorectal screenings are recommended at specific ages. Catching a condition in its early stage dramatically improves outcomes and typically lowers the total cost of care.

title: Mental health and physical health are linked
text: Chronic psychological stress elevates cortisol levels which over time can raise blood pressure, weaken immune response, and disrupt sleep patterns. Regular exercise, strong social connections, and adequate sleep are evidence-based interventions that simultaneously improve both mental and physical wellbeing.

title: Telemedicine expands healthcare access
text: Telemedicine lets patients consult physicians by video or phone without traveling to a clinic. It is especially valuable in rural and underserved areas where specialists are scarce. Common uses include post-surgery follow-ups, prescription renewals, and ongoing mental health therapy sessions.
